Los Angeles, Calif. 90040

Leo Rangell  

Man advances his knowledge in two directions. With the electron microscope he narrows down past the cell towards the molecule and atom. Standing next to a space ship, with the widest angle ever achieved by man, he can now see the earth as a globe. Both views have one thing in common: neither see a man.

Psychoanalysis was the breakthrough which provided a lens to specifically see the size of a man. Sitting alongside a human mind the psychoanalyst sees in both directions as well, how a man does, and does not, look inwards, and why he reaches for the sky. In sifting what I would say at the brief moment of this valedictory, I have chosen to take a long view, of the forest rather than a tree, to look back at the state of our science from the beginning and to bring it up to this current pressing moment in our history. I also wish to relate our field to its neighbouring terrain. Contrary to opinions often held, an analyst is never without seeing and being exquisitely in touch with the outside world. But his is a unique view, an inside-out one, through the intrapsychic process in man.

Four years as President of this International Association results, to put it mildly, in a stimulus overload. From this veritable mountain of interpersonal exchange—I am tempted to say barrage—'perspective' will be my unifying theme. Many of our problems stem from a failure to keep in mind sizes, the relative proportions of elements with which we deal.

Approaching the end of its first century of existence, psychoanalysis has undergone an uneven, stormy, never-uninteresting history during its eventful life. Stamping its time as 'the century of Freud', it shared the history of the 20th century: expansion, diffuse application, use and misuse, explosion, disaster.

My talk will be divided into three sections, the first two short, the third longer.

THE ANALYSIS OF LIFE

From the point of view of clinical practice, psychoanalysis over the decades has moved both imperceptibly and yet with steady increments from the analysis of symptoms to character to the analysis almost of life itself. The relation between the analysis of the Rat Man or the Wolf Man to a psychoanalysis of today is as the relationship of the first electric bulb to Times Square or Piccadilly—or of Edison's first gramophone to a modern sound studio.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychological Conflict and the Structural Model: Some Clinical and Theoretical Implications

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler

Joseph Sandler
The Hampstead Child-Therapy Clinic
21 Maresfield Gardens
London NW3

Joseph Sandler  

I. INTRODUCTION

The concept of conflict is one which has been central to theory and technique from the beginning of psychoanalysis. The present paper examines certain aspects of the theory of conflict with special reference to the relationship between psychological theory and clinical practice. It will be suggested in what follows that with the formulation of the structural theory (Freud, 1923), (1926) a divergence between clinical psychoanalytic practice and psychoanalytic theory has appeared.

Following a short review of the history of the idea of conflict in Freud's writings the view will be put forward in the latter part of the paper that the by now traditional 'structural' view of conflict between the various psychic agencies is insufficient to conceptualize conflict as we infer it clinically. This is not in itself a new idea (cf. Nemiah, 1963); (Rangell, 1963a), (1963b), but a conceptualization of an aspect of conflict will be considered here which may help to make our theoretical formulations more congruent with psychoanalytic observations, practice and technique. The view will be put forward that an essential element in intrapsychic conflict is what can be called the unconscious peremptory urge.1 By 'peremptory' in this context I mean that quality of compellingness and urgency, of being 'automatic', of being driven by a force, which we usually associate with instinctual wishes or their more direct derivatives. However, this so-called peremptory tendency is not confined to the id, but may be regarded as a function of various aspects of the apparatus. The concept embraces derivatives of instinctual impulses, but includes as well compelling urges of a different, non-instinctual character, as for example the automatic and impelling unconscious tendency for an adult to use a childhood defensive manoeuvre in particular circumstances, or the automatic and imperative ego response of anger or rage in circumstances of externally aroused frustration. The peremptory urge may be largely composed of instinctual elements in the present, or have been derived from them in the past, but it is a mistake to equate all peremptory urges with instinctual impulses alone. It will be suggested, later in this paper, that conflict can be more profitably viewed by considering a whole class of peremptory urges where we may have previously considered unconscious instinctual impulses alone. Moreover (to continue this introductory summary), the peremptory urge which arouses conflict may have been acceptable to the individual at an earlier time in his life, but may have later become relatively ego-dystonic for one reason or another, i.e. it brings into play 'checking', 'delaying' or 'inhibitory' tendencies evoked by a signal of danger. The type of conflict considered here may perhaps be conceptualized more adequately by taking into account certain reformulations of the structural model, and these will be described later in the light of relevant previous work, notably that which has emerged from the Hampstead Psychoanalytic Index. This includes, in particular, the formulations regarding the concept of 'persistence' (Sandler & Joffe, 1967)

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Perversion, Idealization and Sublimation

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel
41 rue de Verneuil
75007 Paris

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel  

Freud (1914) introduced the ego-ideal along with narcissism into psychoanalytic theory and thereby made it the heir to primary narcissism. Man, unable to give up a satisfaction he has once enjoyed, 'is not willing to forgo the narcissistic perfection of his childhood' and 'seeks to recover it in the new form of an ego-ideal. What he projects before him as his ideal is the substitute for the lost narcissism of his childhood in which he was his own ideal'.

In fact, as Freud indicated in a footnote to 'Instincts and their Vicissitudes' (1915), the rupture of the primal narcissistic state is tied to the subject's being powerless to help himself, a helplessness that forces him to recognize the non-self, the object, after a period of hallucinatory gratification of desire. The object becomes cathected with the lost omnipotence of the subject. We know that the narcissism which man 'projected before him' is precisely the formation of the ego ideal, and will subsequently cathect other objects and will, in the case of the boy, direct itself toward the father-figure during the oedipal phase. Without going into the details of this development, we can state that it is a long journey from the moment when the subject is his own ideal to the moment when he gives over his narcissism to his homosexual object, the father, who becomes his model, in other words his projected itinerary for identification.

To my mind, the stumbling-blocks to this development of the ego-ideal help to illuminate the relationship between the ego-ideal and the overall development of the individual. In this respect the example of the sexual pervert seems particularly significant.

Within the aetiology of perversions it has often been noted that the mother frequently assumes a seductive and collusive attitude towards the child (Bak, 1968). My own clinical experience points exactly in this direction. Sexual perverts say with ease, 'I didn't have to take my father's place, I always had it' or else they tell how their mother took them to bed with her while father slept in the dining-room; or again they remember scenes where their mother undressed in front of them, kissed them on the mouth, manifested a constant adoration expressed through caresses, tender words and an emotional intimacy allied to unusual physical promiscuity. These intense exchanges between mother and son seem to occur in a closed circuit from which the father is excluded. What seems important to me in terms of our topic is that it appears that the mother fostered an illusion in making the son believe that, with his infantile sexuality, he was a perfect partner for her, and therefore had nothing to envy in his father, thus arresting his development. His ego-ideal, instead of directing itself towards the genital father and his penis, remains henceforth attached to a pregenital model.

[[RunningHead]]

Further Contributions to the Treatment of Narcissistic Personalities

Otto Kernberg

Otto Kernberg

Otto F. Kernberg
47 Walworth Ave.
Searsdale, New York 10583

Otto Kernberg  

This paper continues my earlier studies of the diagnosis and psychoanalytic treatment of a specific constellation of character pathology, that of the narcissistic personality (Kernberg, 1967), (1970a), (1971a). In recent years, a consensus has been gradually developing regarding the definition of this pathological character structure and the indication of psychoanalysis as the treatment of choice (Jacobson, 1964); (P. Kernberg, 1971); (Kohut, 1966), (1968), (1971); (Rosenfeld, 1964); (Tartakoff, 1966); (E. Ticho, 1970); (van der Waals, 1965). However, despite the evolving agreement about the descriptive, clinical characteristics of this constellation, divergent views have developed regarding the underlying metapsychological assumptions and the optimal technical approach within a psychoanalytic modality of treatment. In particular, Kohut's approach to the psychoanalytic treatment of narcissistic personality disorders (1971) is very different from the approach I outlined in an earlier paper (Kernberg, 1970a), which is more closely related to the views of Abraham (1919), Jacobson (1964), Riviere (1936), Rosenfeld (1964), Tartakoff (1966) and van der Waals (1965). Therefore, in this paper I will focus particularly on those aspects of my approach to the understanding and treatment of narcissistic personalities which highlight agreements and disagreements with Kohut's approach.

CLINICAL CHARACTERISTICS OF THE NARCISSISTIC PERSONALITY AS A SPECIFIC TYPE OF CHARACTER PATHOLOGY

With respect to clinical characteristics, there is agreement between Kohut's view and that of the other authors whom I have mentioned as representing an alternative view and myself. I describe patients with narcissistic personalities as presenting excessive self-absorption usually coinciding with a superficially smooth and effective social adaptation, but with serious distortions in their internal relationships with other people. They present various combinations of intense ambitiousness, grandiose fantasies, feelings of inferiority, and overdependence on external admiration and acclaim. Along with feelings of boredom and emptiness, and continuous search for gratification of strivings for brilliance, wealth, power and beauty, there are serious deficiencies in their capacity to love and to be concerned about others. This lack of capacity for empathic understanding of others often comes as a surprise considering their superficially appropriate social adjustment. Chronic uncertainty and dissatisfaction about themselves, conscious or unconscious exploitiveness and ruthlessness towards others are also characteristics of these patients. Perhaps one difference in my description from that derived from Kohut's work is my stress on the pathological nature of their internalized object relations, regardless of the superficially adaptive behaviour of many of these patients. In addition, I stress the presence of chronic, intense envy, and defences against such envy, particularly devaluation, omnipotent control and narcissistic withdrawal, as major characteristics of their emotional life.

responseSet.title: A Discussion of the Paper by Claude Le Guen on 'The Formation of the Transference'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by Claude Le Guen on 'The Formation of the Transference'

A. Béjarano

A. Béjarano

A. Béjarano
15 rue de l'Estrapade
75005 Paris

A. Béjarano  

What particularly pleased me about Le Guen's paper (this issue) is that it reflected my own interest in the countertransference. I found Le Guen well prepared to defend the current movement towards promoting and rehabilitating the countertransference, which not only has been treated off handedly in psychoanalytic theory and practice but has also been held in contempt; since it has been repressed, it has remained unmastered.

This is one of the main qualities of Le Guen's paper and I hope that the discussion will do justice to it, because discussions at this Congress seem to indicate (and I hope that I am wrong) that the countertransference is still regarded by participants as a 'bad object'.

But Le Guen's paper contains other interesting things. Firstly, Le Guen, within the framework of his discussion, poses questions about the countertransference. Regarding a stumbling-block which he encountered during treatment, he had the courage to seek out the truth about himself, that of his patient, and, ultimately, the truth of their work together, in their highly specific relationship—a relationship which is characteristic of any psychoanalytic treatment. He was, above all, courageous enough to talk to us about it. In doing so, he was able to show us that it is possible and desirable to leave the comfort of one's armchair and to ask oneself questions concerning this (Theban) throne, and thus escape the somewhat reassuring (but, in fact, alienating) frame of reference that tends to leave the patient carrying the full burden of the resistance, and to believe, with a benevolent intention, in the perfect neutrality of the analyst.

Le Guen shows us through an example of a clinical experience—the patient's as well as his own—that analysis does not mean talking about the patient in the third person, tantamount to excluding the analyst from the realm of the experience of treatment, but that analysis is very much, in a strict sense, an interrelationship.

If the transference is in the patient's case not only the most efficient weapon of the resistance but also the motivating force of the treatment, and is the area, according to Freud, where victory must be achieved, this is equally true in terms of the countertransference: it is also the analyst's most powerful weapon in the resistance (especially if he denies or ignores its existence); but once it is recognized and mastered, it becomes the most sure tool for situating and interpreting the transference, and consequently for causing treatment to progress.

[[RunningHead]]

The Difficulties of the Training Analyst in the Training Analysis

Arnold Pfeffer

Arnold Pfeffer

Arnold Z. Pfeffer
39 East 74th Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Arnold Pfeffer  

This is a report of the Pre-Congress meeting of the International Psycho-Analytical Association in Paris, July 1973. The topic was 'The Training Analyst and the Difficulties in the Psychoanalytical Training Situation'. The four preceding Pre-Congresses concerned themselves with the following aspects of psychoanalytic education: selection of candidates; the training analysis; supervision; and the assessment of candidates' progress.

Approximately 150 training analysts first met to hear and discuss an introductory paper by Dr A. Limentani of London. Then they divided into ten discussion groups. The participants met for three sessions, a total of five hours. This was followed by a plenary session at which Mrs Joyce McDougall of Paris and Dr H. S. Klein of London presented summaries of the ten group discussions. I am grateful to both of them for their excellent reports which have contributed much to the present report which is, with additions, essentially the same as the report delivered to the Congress meeting that followed. In this brief report it will only be possible to summarize the main themes and some of the minority views, and I hope that the discussion participants, chairmen and secretaries of groups will understand and accept this limitation.

The full title of the Pre-Congress meeting was: 'The Training Analyst and the Difficulties in the Training Psychoanalytical Situation: Difficulties Arising from the Training Organization, the Training Analyst and the Candidate'. This title, long and somewhat complicated, required close scrutiny to determine its central intention and main focus: difficulties of the training analyst in the training analysis. This was admirably achieved in the opening talk outlining the many issues involved and in the discussion which followed.

Dr Limentani's paper is published in this issue of the Journal, and will not be summarized here.

In the discussion that immediately followed Dr Limentani's paper, it was agreed that the main point of interest was the training analyst and his difficulties in the training analysis. All analyses have, for the analyst, their particular problems and difficulties: however, the concern of the present meeting was with the special difficulties of the analyst in the training situation, and, either explicitly or implicitly, in comparison with therapeutic analyses.

responseSet.title: The Body Image in Hysterical Psychosis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Body Image in Hysterical Psychosis

Gisela Pankow

Gisela Pankow

Gisela W. Pankow
6ter Bd. Delessert
75016 Paris

Gisela Pankow  

It is not usual at a scientific congress to start with images, but I wish only to use them as metaphors to introduce and make clear my position of research in relation to psychosis. (1) I shall try to conceptualize psychosis authentically, in statu nascendi, through the patient's own language, and (2) I shall try to explain the data of psychosis, avoiding extrapolations from the structure of neurosis, and therefore going from 'the inside' to 'the outside' and not remaining in 'the underwood border' of an impenetrable, formerly burnt and deforestated virgin forest which can sometimes block all access to 'the inside'. The metaphor of the virgin forest is intended to show the specific character of this terra incognita which in German is called Fremdheit: that which is forever foreign and cannot be grasped by mere extrapolation from ordinary life; one must have the courage to recognize the gap which separates us from psychosis.

The attempts to understand psychosis without bridging this gap have resulted in the massive nosography inherited from classical psychiatry. But 'the inside' method is that of personal involvement, of daring to take the patient 'by the hand' and accompany him in his 'descent into hell'.

Without the knowledge of the structures of the unconscious which Freud developed as an instrument for the treatment of neurosis, the analytical approach to psychosis would not have been possible. There is indeed only one unconscious. Several years ago, I suggested the following formula: 'Neurosis and psychosis differ from each other in that the fundamental structures which find symbolic representation in language and which contain the precipitates of the earliest body experiences are destroyed in psychosis and distorted in neurosis' (Pankow, 1960, p. 274).

THE BODY IMAGE AS A SYMBOLIZING FUNCTION

For some 20 years, I have based my analytical approach to the treatment of psychosis and certain psychosomatic disorders on the body image, and have been able to demonstrate clinically that the zones of destruction in the body image of psychotic patients correspond to the zones of destruction in their family structures. Thus I have defined the body image according to two fundamental functions which are symbolizing functions: functions which permit first the recognition of a dynamic link between the part and the whole of the body (first fundamental function of the body image), and, over and above the external outline of such a dynamic link, a recognition of its content and meaning (second fundamental function of the body image) (Pankow, 1960), (1972).

<autaff><addr><ln><cs lang="en" style="AuthorName">Peter Hartocollis</cs></ln><ln>The Menninger Foundation</ln><ln>Box 829</ln><ln>Topeka, Kansas 66601</ln></addr></autaff></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Mysticism and Violence: The Case of Nikos Kazantzakis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mysticism and Violence: The Case of Nikos Kazantzakis

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis

Peter Hartocollis
The Menninger Foundation
Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Peter Hartocollis  

As the violence of the world around him was approaching its climax and barely a year before he was overcome by the violence of cancer and old age, Freud (1938) entered these seemingly irrelevant thoughts in his notebook: 'Mysticism is the obscure self-perception of the realm outside the ego, of the id' (p. 300). Freud was not a mystic, but his interest in mysticism and the occult is well documented (Jones, 1957). That he should turn his attention to mysticism at this particular point of his life is not surprising either, for mysticism and the search for a mystical experience that Freud tried to explain with this late entry in his notebook are inherent in the yearning for peace and love that men besieged by violence and death might be expected to feel.

People wish for peace when they are either afraid or tired of war. But mystics and mystical movements do not usually appear in the midst of all-out wars, when violence and privation are upon everybody. Mysticism seems to be a manifestation of conflict with one's own aggression rather than with the violence of the outside world. The presence of external aggression, if directly threatening the individual, is not likely to produce a mystical reaction but, rather, fear, escape, or violence in self-defence. The mystical urge for peace and love develops in the context of a nominally peaceful society that promotes and vicariously enjoys violence, offering personal immunity from violence but leaving the impression that it is futile to oppose it. Such conflictual external circumstances, reflecting a similar internal conflict, are likely to promote the wish for a mystical experience, which may provide peace and love for the individual concerned apart from the society in which he originates.

The search for peace and love through mysticism, motivated by the wish to resolve one's conflict over his own potential for violence, does not necessarily lead to a peaceful, conflictfree mystical experience. In fact, quite often, the effort to induce the regressive conditions necessary for a mystical experience results in the flooding of one's ego with violent wishes, bringing about a psychotic episode, a 'demonic' rather than 'religious' experience or, if it involves drugs, a 'bad trip' rather than the desired 'psychedelic' experience. I have discussed this dangerous possibility elsewhere, documenting it with a number of characteristic clinical examples (Hartocollis, 1971). In the present paper I will try to show how a well-known psychosomatic syndrome, identified in the literature of mysticism with the struggle of ascetics against sexuality, can be explained better as a manifestation of the mystic's struggle against his own internal violence. In support of this hypothesis, I will present the case of Nikos Kazantzakis, whose life and writings were guided by mystical principles and a tireless search for love and peace.

responseSet.title: Stealing, Revenge and the Monte Cristo Complex
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Stealing, Revenge and the Monte Cristo Complex

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco
Department of Psychiatry, Harbor General Hospital
Torrance, Calif. 90509

Pietro Castelnuovo-Tedesco  

A vertigo seized Edmond; he cocked his gun and laid it beside him. He then closed his eyes as children do in order to perceive in the shining night of their imagination more stars than are visible in the firmament; then he reopened them, and stood motionless with amazement. Three compartments divided the coffer. In the first blazed piles of golden coin; in the second, bars of unpolished gold …; in the third, … handfuls of diamonds, pearls and rubies … After having touched, felt and examined these treasures, Edmond rushed through the caverns like a man seized with frenzy; he leaped on a rock, from whence he could behold the sea. He was alone. Alone with these countless, these unheard-of treasures! Was he awake, or was it but a dream? ALEXANDRE DUMAS, The Count of Monte Cristo, ch. 25

It is permissible to surmise that if Edmond Dantès, later the Count of Monte Cristo, had been a real person, a living being rather than a character in a 19th-century romantic novel, he might have been known as a thief. This statement may seem startling, or at least puzzling at first, since most of us remember Monte Cristo from our youth as the epitome of moral righteousness. In fact, it was precisely this quality that made him so attractive to the reader, then under the sway of the natural idealism of adolescence. How could one, at that age, not admire his steadfastness in pursuit of wrongdoers, his single-mindedness in tracking down those who had hurt him and his persistence in obtaining, finally, the revenge which was so justly his.

Superficially, the book offers the reader a moral orgy: Right wins out over Evil, despite every conceivable difficulty and barrier which Fate places in the hero's path. The hero seems to show no faltering of energy and no uncertainty of purpose, precisely because he knows that his cause is just; this gives him the strength needed to surmount all obstacles and, indeed, ultimately he (and the reader, vicariously) enjoy the sublime satisfaction of knowing that justice has been rendered and that no wrong has been forgotten or gone unpunished.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Anorexia Nervosa Syndrome

John Sours

John Sours

John A. Sours
2 East 86th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

John Sours  

From a close scrutiny of the literature on anorexia nervosa and from working with anorexia nervosa patients in psychoanalytic therapy, it is apparent that the disorder causes among analysts differences of opinion regarding diagnosis, psychopathogenesis and treatment.

Analysts are often reluctant to treat anorexia nervosa patients. It is one of the few psychosomatic disorders which are life-threatening, for it is associated with an overall mortality rate varying from 7–15 per cent (Sours, 1969). When an anorexia nervosa patient embarks on her relentless pursuit of thinness and absolute control over her body, the emaciation can proceed to cachexia and inanition, sometimes ending in circulatory collapse or cardiac arrest (Ziegler & Sours, 1968). Uncertainties about diagnosis, therapy and the patient's physical health often arise, and the analyst is confronted with the question of whether hospitalization for refeeding and restoration of nutrition and health is necessary (Bruch, 1966); (Crisp, 1968); (Falstein et al., 1956); (Sours, 1973).

I intend first to summarize the literature on anorexia nervosa with emphasis on some issues of diagnosis, treatment and medical management. Then I will draw on my own experience with anorexia nervosa patients whom I have treated either in a university hospital or in private practice.

Central to the discussion is the point that anorexia nervosa is not a specific nosological entity with predictable findings and a predictably grave outcome.

I want to emphasize that there is a group of adolescent girls with anorexia nervosa who can be treated with traditional analytic techniques. The other two groups of anorexia nervosa patients, who display regressive dedifferentiation, possible loss of self–object separation and mental self–body–self differentiation and reactivation of archaic ego states, frequently first require auxiliary medical management. Nevertheless, once their relentless starvation stops and life is no longer endangered, analytic therapy can often be used for even these patients (Sours, 1973); (Thomä, 1967).

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by E. L. Goldberg, W. A. Myers and I. Zeifman on 'Some Observations on Three Interracial Analyses'

Edward Joseph

Edward Joseph

Edward D. Joseph
Department of Psychiatry, Mount Sinai Hospital
11 East 100th Street, New York, N.Y. 10029

Edward Joseph  

To say that I enjoyed and appreciated the opportunity to read the paper by Dr Goldberg and his colleagues (this issue), and now to discuss it, is to understate the situation. I think this paper is of importance in a number of ways. Therefore the opportunity to talk about it is one I am eager to accept.

Psychoanalysis has been under criticism ever since its inception. The critics present a variety of reasons for their opposition to psychoanalytic findings and theory. In the past years, particularly in the United States, some of the criticism has been directed at the fact that psychoanalysis, as a therapy and as a theory, is derived from, and applicable to, only the white middle-class. These critics state that, while the findings are interesting, the therapy is questionable, but since it derived from the study of Viennese middle-class patients, it might at the most have some application to their equivalent categories in the United States or in other countries, but not to individuals of a mixed culture or a different racial background. To some extent, the critics have had a field day, since, given the nature of psychoanalytic practice, there have not been many analyses of blacks or of other individuals from the so-called disadvantaged or lower socio-economic levels.

A widely accepted psychological study suggests that the form of treatment offered to an individual depends as much on his socio-economic status as on the nature of his illness. Their findings purport to demonstrate that white upper- and middle-class patients tend to be directed and accepted into more psychotherapeutically orientated treatment modalities, particularly psychoanalysis, while Caucasian and black individuals of a lower socio-economic status tend to be directed into avenues of therapy that emphasize an organic or drug-orientated approach. But that these findings are not as valid as was claimed by Hollingshead & Redlich (1960) has been indicated by other individuals, whose work in a number of clinics and hospitals has brought them into contact with individuals of a lower socio-economic status, and who have found that these patients are as amenable and approachable by the usual psychodynamically orientated psychotherapy as any other group (Kaufman, 1964).

responseSet.title: A Discussion of the Paper by René Major on 'The Revolution of Hysteria'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by René Major on 'The Revolution of Hysteria'

Christian David

Christian David

Christian David
77 rue de Lille
75007 Paris

Christian David  

With his characteristic elegance and subtlety, René Major (this issue) has drawn his insights from the deepest meanings of hysteria and has found a new and revealing perspective on its history and dynamics. He convincingly evokes for us how Freud began by restoring to the hysteric his own language, while simultaneously giving back to language 'its own internal hysteria'; how he was thus, from the very beginning, accomplishing a revolution which, even before it became the psychoanalytic revolution, appears retrospectively as 'the revolution of hysteria'. And, with only the appearance of paradox, Major makes coincide the birth and the death of hysteria—instantaneous revolution; for, in fact, the observation of hysterics led Freud, he says, 'to produce a theory which abolishes hysteria' and with it the alienating relation which distinguishes it. This abolition, which originated in the break with Charcot's conception of language, was confirmed by the invention of the psychoanalytic method and situation: barring a failure in listening on the part of the therapist, 'there is no longer a hysteric in the analytic situation'. This latter, in uncovering unconscious fantasies, nullifies their effects, namely the hysterical symptoms and their utilization for the ends of domination—thus revolution in a double sense of the term. Just as phantoms and spectres vanish with the day, so the discovery of the unconscious (the affirmation of a sense where before only nonsense had been seen) is dissipated, almost as if by magic, the opaqueness of the convulsion and the cry. And, in Freud's perspective during 1890 and 1891, that of the original dominance of the acoustic image over the other elements which constitute the word, or, in other terms, a definitive primacy of the heard over the seen (the same point of view as that chosen by Major to elaborate his own conception), these two, these cries and these convulsions, are constrained to transform themselves into words. For the mise en scène (the visual), which Charcot favoured, Freud substituted the mise en mots of analysis; a plastic semiology gives way to an auditory one where there is no longer a gap between the interior and the exterior, between the perceived and the thought. In the last analysis, then, it is sufficient to know how to spot and understand the 'constituent linguistic elements which represent the drive' (to be sure, starting off from a singular discourse and without betraying that singularity). This rather intellectualized conception of the genius of psychoanalysis, however, is tempered for Major by the conviction—which I can only share—that one is never finished with hysteria, a genuine Hydra of Lerna. We may drive it back, as Freud did, behind a new border, but it will not fail to reappear. Revolution around an axis, though not circular but spiral, for the point of reappearance does not coincide with the Freudian point of departure. As light and fragile as this difference can sometimes appear, it is the mark of a progression, a progress of knowledge. The ruses of the unconscious, such as those of Hegelian reason, cannot completely annul—at least so far as science is concerned—the conquests due to the development of awareness, to the taking of consciousness. If hysteria came back, eternally came back, it would be after a metamorphosis; today it would have to 'flow in the forms' which Freud imposed on it.

[[RunningHead]]

Adoption Trauma: Oedipus Myth/clinical Reality

Luis Feder

Luis Feder

Luis Feder
Corregidores 1429
Mexico 10, D.F., Mexico City

Luis Feder  

The manifest Oedipus myth is the case history of an adopted child. The ubiquity of both myth and complex is associated with Freud's concept of the 'family romance' (1909). The adoption trauma as a concept, as a syndrome and a universal trend follows the clinical sequence tabulated below. It is identical with Rank's 'average myth' (cf. Freud, 1939) and with the Oedipus myth. The 200 cases (see note at the end of this paper) which form the basis for this paper follow the same typical pattern. The traumatic vicissitudes of adoption as outlined here, attempt to integrate (from the points of view of both time sequence and psychological significance) previously neglected, earlier psychogenic components (Feder, 1965a), (1965b), (Calef, 1968).

THE CLINICAL SEQUENCE OF THE ADOPTION TRAUMA

i. Ambivalent, conflictual preconceptive coupling, leading to

ii. unwanted pregnancy, which causes

iii. somatization, abortions (fantasied as well as attempted but frustrated), causing in turn

iv. traumatic birth, resulting from the attitudes of a rejecting, filicidal, foeticidal mother. The traumatic impact of this on the child and of the mother's attitudes and subsequent abandonment of the child, leads to

v. an adoption agent (or agency) 'romance' which is seldom professional, particularly because of unavailable diagnostic criteria. This often reinforces scotomata towards adoptive motivations, leading to

vi. adoptive parents whose profound motivations are unknown. These motivations fluctuate 'pregnancy induction', 'sterility reparation', 'guilty-submissive' in content. The adoptive parents are often psychotics or perverts engaged in triangular mutual sado-masochistic exploitation, masked by

vii. pre-adoptive idealization, on the part of the adoptive parents, followed by

viii. a post-idyllic period, marked by conflict, and possibly the subsequent returning, substitution or rejection of the child (with infanticidal wishes). This may bring about, in the child,

ix. an 'adopted child' pathology, which can flower into narcissistic character disorder, psychotic episodes, delinquency, homosexuality, fantasied or attempted suicide, incest, homicide, fratricide, murder of one or both adoptive parents, and to patricide or matricide.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Notes on the Psychical Roots of Religion, with Particular Reference to the Development of Western Christianity

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber
16 Elsworthy Road
London, N.W.3

John Klauber  

Freud's genius lay in his understanding of the importance of fantasy formation for the fate of the individual. But his grasp of its importance for society in terms of the mythopoetic function of religion was much weaker.

In the least convincing strand in Freud's writings on religion he saw it as 'patently infantile' and 'the obsessional neurosis of mankind'. However, he became dissatisfied with his failure in 'The Future of an Illusion' (1927) to account for the high value set on religion by the individual and by society, and tried to remedy it in 'Civilization and its Discontents' (1930) by suggesting that the glory of God might have its psychical roots in the child's impressions of the power of his father. He confessed himself puzzled by the psychical roots of the great mother deities who seemed everywhere to have preceded the father gods. The most satisfactory strand in Freud's views on religion was his recognition of the importance of myth for the transmission of culture. This was expounded particularly in his last work ('Moses and Monotheism', 1939), but his whole theory of society ('Totem and Taboo', 1913) was a modern version of the theory of the social contract making religion its cement. The psychical roots of Christianity, the religion which has dominated our culture for sixteen centuries, Freud virtually ignored; and in spite of notable exceptions, such as Erich Fromm (1963) and Theodor Reik (1958), most psychoanalysts have done the same since.

The importance of religion as a cement of society seems to be illustrated by the high social status conceded to religious organizations and leaders—at least until recently. And religious faith has often been counted as a criterion of the reliability and cohesion of an individual's character. Since the intelligence of a child of five may be sufficient to expose the logical weakness of a religious myth, as Freud showed in the Wolf Man story (1918), some explanation of the value placed on religion is clearly required. In this connexion Arlow (1961) has emphasized the power of the instinctual wish expressed in the myth to attract identification with the hero in the ego.

responseSet.title: Some Remarks on the Present State of Psychoanalysis in France
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Some Remarks on the Present State of Psychoanalysis in France

Evelyne Kestemberg

Evelyne Kestemberg

Evelyne Kestemberg
6 rue Friant
75014 Paris

Evelyne Kestemberg  

It is a great pleasure for me to welcome you here today on behalf of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society.

Since the International Congress held in Paris 16 years ago, psychoanalysis has changed, perhaps even substantially, in theory as well as in practice. Some of these changes can be regarded with satisfaction, while others give rise to some anxiety. One reason for satisfaction is undoubtedly the fact that there are in Paris two psychoanalytic Societies to welcome you, both of which have in common the desire to develop Freud's work along strict lines. Like all of you here, both these Societies are heirs to Freud's revolutionary contribution which has so immeasurably enriched human thought—a contribution whose full meaning we may still not have fully appreciated—and both of them fulfil their duty as trustees in their own way. So, these two Societies—joined together as part of the International Psycho-Analytical Association—see their development as following a rhythm which, fortunately without being spectacular, is that of a team whose speed is constant, mobile and swift enough to ensure continuous progress and security in the face of various obstacles.

Moreover, and this is our second reason to be satisfied, this progress and security are due to the quality of the young psychoanalysts that we have in training. Most of them show signs of such quality of thought, even creativity, and an eager desire to learn that, thinking of what we were when we began and comparing our ignorance then with their knowledge now, it is a deep pleasure to see how much ground has been gained. During all these years, successive contributions have enriched the theory and practice of psychoanalysis and have allowed us to better outline its difficulties, prospects and problems still not resolved. Today our young colleagues successfully assimilate them and use them in their daily experience and practice, as well as in their theory. Besides, the patients themselves have changed and the clinical experience of psychoanalysis ranges over wider and more complex fields than even 15 years ago. From my point of view, these changes in symptomatology, or rather these new expressions of the psychic apparatus, deserve theoretical investigation. This would be irrelevant here, so I will not dwell on it, but pass on to the third reason for our satisfaction.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Peculiar Form of Resistance to Psychoanalytical Treatment

Helena Vianna

Helena Vianna

Helena Besserman Vianna
Anita Garibaldi 43 apt. 502
Rio de Janeiro—GB 07, Brazil

Helena Vianna  

SUMMARY

The author presents a summary of the important papers in the psychoanalytical literature dedicated to the study of the negative therapeutic reaction. These papers are those she used to characterize certain rules and theoretical–practical knowledge that gave direction to this present paper. She then describes a patient with a disturbance of sleep rhythm, linked with a strong narcissistic structure, and considers her as a case of negative therapeutic reaction.

Without rejecting the patient's different aspects, the author desires to emphasize that, in this case, the contact of the patient with his infantile parts, experienced as dependent and weak before his love objects, released a form of negative therapeutic reaction, which was expressed in the form of a sleep disturbance. In work with this type of patient, the author stresses the fundamental importance of the analysis of envy, the defences used against the feeling of envy, and of aggressiveness, as a form of work that showed itself adequate and useful in obtaining the slow and gradual collaboration and integration of the patient.

A Discussion of the Paper by Joseph Sandler on 'Psychological Conflict and the Structural Model: Some Clinical and Theoretical Implications'

Samuel Abrams

Samuel Abrams

Samuel Abrams
25 East 83rd Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Samuel Abrams  

SUMMARY

Dr Sandler's observations on the existence of various imperatives which require analytic engagement are noted. His term 'unconscious peremptory urge' and his views may have some pedagogic value but they raise a conceptual problem, a clinical matter, and a theoretical issue. Conceptually, his label runs the risk of blurring certain fundamental psychoanalytic distinctive ideas; clinically, the 'equivalent' implied in his proposal may be unjustified; and theoretically, the conceptual opportunities inherent in our economic propositions appear to have been overlooked.

A Discussion of the Paper by Ishak Ramzy on 'How the Mind of the Psycho-Analyst Works'

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth Calder

Kenneth T. Calder
110 E. 78th. Street
New York, N.Y. 10021

Kenneth Calder  

There is a charming and profound observation recorded in a book, Charlotte's Web, by the American humorist, E. B. White, which reads as follows: 'It is deeply satisfying to win a prize in front of a lot of people.' I wish to offer a prize to Dr Ramzy in front of the 'lot of people' gathered here today. My prize is in the form of a compliment: I think Dr Ramzy has written a scholarly, interesting and useful paper (this issue), well worth reading and well worth discussing.

It is clear to me that I agree with him in most of his conclusions: his support of the scientific method, his interest in research and his description of the use of logic in analysis. Whether or not I agree with him in all areas depends on our definitions, as I will now try to detail.

At one point, Dr Ramzy notes that 'psycho-analysis as a method can be defined as a science which deals with the errors of the human mind'. At another point he states that 'psycho-analysis as a clinical method of investigation strictly adheres to the common logical canons …'. Perhaps we view these matters similarly, but I wish to give examples which imply to me that analysis deals not alone with 'errors of the human mind' but also with activities of the human mind which are not errors and the comprehension of which obliges the analyst to use methods 'outside logical canons'. I refer here to the use of empathy, intuition and, as a general category, regression in the service of the ego. My examples come from data-collecting activities by the analyst, but I will refer also to data evaluation by the analyst.

It is especially in the area of data collecting, I believe, that the analyst departs from the model of the logician or the computer. I agree that analysts employ methods of logic such as contiguity, similarity, contrast and also repetition, omissions, analogies, intensities, etc. But if we add to his role as logician, the opportunities made possible by regression in the service of the ego, I believe we get a better picture of the breadth of the analyst's data collecting.

How the Mind of the Psychoanalyst Works: An Essay on Psychoanalytic Inference

Ishak Ramzy

Ishak Ramzy

Ishak Ramzy
The Menninger Foundation
Box 829
Topeka, Kansas 66601

Ishak Ramzy  

Unbelievable as it may sound, in the whole vast library of psychoanalysis—clinical, theoretical, technical or applied—there are hardly any references which outline the logical guidelines or the methodological rules which the analyst follows in order to understand his patient. Works on psychoanalytic technique are replete with the rules of therapy—with the timing, the types and the ways of formulating the comments which the analyst can convey to his analysand. However, the analyst's interpretations can only be derived from his own understanding of the patient's story and frequently they are only a part of the inferences the analyst makes first in his own mind as he listens along, hour after hour, to what the patient talks about.

Dispersed in Freud's monumental works, especially in his earlier clinical writings, can only be found a few passages where he gives an occasional description of how he reasoned out what he observed, or statements in misleadingly simple language of one or the other logical tenets he followed in reaching his conclusions. As time went by, the various theoretical constructs and working hypotheses developed by generations of psychoanalysts became intermingled within the clinical accounts and taken for granted as if they were self-evident facts which needed no further proof or explanation.

A careful scrutiny of the literature would turn up, of course, several contributions which deal directly or indirectly with the psychoanalytic method. They correctly claim that it is a careful investigation of the human mental processes, which proceeds in a rational and objective manner and follows strict methodological rules that adhere to the accepted canons of the scientific method. How this meticulous investigation is conducted or how this rational and objective method proceeds or what canons it follows are issues which are always left unsaid and undefined or at most but vaguely sketched or taken for granted.

Recognizing the lack of any established canons for the interpretation of clinical observations, Rapaport once made a preliminary attempt in the 1940s to initiate a study of psychoanalytic methodology. Though blazing a new trail, Rapaport's attempt contained errors of fact and definition, and mixtures of levels of discourse. Realizing the shortcomings of his contribution, published posthumously (1967), Rapaport declined to put it into print during his lifetime, saying: 'I feel that my thinking about this matter is much more in a chaos. Maybe the day will come when I can put together the methodological, the epistemological and the conceptual treatment of these issues. That day, however, seems to me right now very far off. Frankly the more I fool around with these things, the more ignorant do I find myself.

[[RunningHead]]

Fusion with the Victim and Suicide

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel

Shelley Orgel
152 East Avenue
Norwalk, Connecticut 06851

Shelley Orgel  

I

Anna Freud (1936) describes how a little girl conquered her fear of coming across ghosts in the dark hall. She made all sorts of peculiar gestures as she walked, saying, 'There is no need to be afraid in the hall, you just have to pretend that you are the ghost who might meet you.' The warding off by externalization of the danger of being overwhelmed by the returned dead serves as a beautiful introduction to a paper relating a vicissitude of identification with the aggressor, suicide, and creativity.

Miss Freud describes identification with the aggressor as a 'by no means uncommon stage' in the normal development of the superego. The internalized aggression or criticism, not as yet immediately transformed into self-criticism, is dissociated from the child's own reprehensible activity (Beres, 1958). In other terms, identification with the aggressor implies a defensive ability to separate self and object images by projection of the less neutralized aggressive drives on to external objects at the same time as a sample, as it were, is temporarily taken into the self. It therefore finds a place analogous to 'negation' as a partial denial of unpleasurable reality in the service of protecting the immature ego (Freud, 1925). Externalization of the offence partially spares the immature ego the destructive effects of insufficiently neutralized aggression. True morality begins when the internalized criticism, now embodied in the standards exacted by the superego (whose contents include aggression of varying degrees of neutralization compatible with the ego's ability to mediate discharge or countercathexis), coincides with the ego's perception of its own fault. True morality also implies a stable retention of self–object differentiation, and maintains the object relationship even in the face of threatened aggression by the object or the self. A structured superego is, then, a barrier against suicide, maintaining a balance between identification and projective elements compatible with normal empathy, preventing both object loss and regressive self–object fusion.

Open Forum: An Exchange on Psychoanalytic Issues

Carl Adatto

Carl Adatto

Carl Adatto
6205 Garfield Street
New Orleans, Louisiana 70118

Carl Adatto  

The following is a condensed version of the proceedings of the Forum, which evolved into a lively exchange and presentation of individual points of view.

Dr Rangell opened the meeting with an invitation to the audience to participate in a dialogue with the Panel on any aspect of the issues of the science and art of psychoanalysis. He then read a question submitted to him: At a time when manifest symptom neurosis is not so clear, is the concept of transference neurosis still useful?

Dr Stein considers transference neurosis to be at least as useful now as before and does not know whether there has been a change in symptom neuroses themselves or whether this is due to a change as a result of analysts' looking for and understanding manifestations in character. In cases of acting out—the re-enactment of unconscious infantile fantasies, often of a primitive character, in everyday life—the patient's behaviour outside the analysis may become relatively free of acting out and brought into the analysis in the ideal analytic situation. Analysis of the acting out starts at this point.

Dr Pasche stated that transference neurosis happens frequently in analyses and that without it an analysis would not have much chance of succeeding. He distinguished resistance to the transference and the transference neurosis, and the splitting of the ego in cases of hysteria—the establishment of distance in relationship to what the patient is feeling. There is a qualitative difference between transference neurosis and the resistance and theatrical aspects of such patients.

Dr Sandler described the process of the formation of the transference neurosis in which the internal or internalized conflict, which enters into the formation of the neurosis or pathological aspects of character, gets externalized in the analytic situation so that there is a conflict, not within the patient, but externalized so that it is between the patient and analyst. Concurrently we get a concentration of emotional forces on the person of the analyst, with relative amelioration and relative withdrawal of involvement outside, so that there is a certain improvement outside. He does not think that it is an ideal which we always reach, and that we must consider not only short-term aims of the analysis, but also the future of the patient after he leaves analysis. One of the ways of getting change is through the analysis of the transference neurosis, but if we do not get it, we may have to use other methods. With the development of more emphasis on character, we find a certain resistance on the part of the patient to the externalization in the analytic situation. It does not always happen that we can change a characterological problem into a neurotic one, and we may have to depart from classical analytic technique. Dr Sandler feels that the question is not whether transference neurosis is no longer useful—of course, it is as useful—but whether we can get it. Discomfort caused by a character trait is not as great as discomfort caused by a neurotic symptom, with the result that many analyses have to be conducted without a full-blown transference neurosis. He believes that often one can get good results in these cases, comparable, for example, with the results obtained in the analysis of children who are usually living with their real parents.

Claude Le Guen

Claude Le Guen

Claude Le Guen
62 Boulevard du Montparnasse
Paris 15

Claude Le Guen  

Oedipus: In defending Laius, I am also serving myself. SOPHOCLES

Laius had really only himself to blame. He should have heeded the gods' warning and used contraception. But no, this blockhead impregnated Jocasta and, of course, had the son that he dreaded. He was then fated. After a bungled abortion, infanticide was the only solution.

We may ask why Laius did not do the job himself. It needed only a stab of his dagger to pierce Oedipus' heart, but two to perforate his heels. Was this a case of bad conscience? However that may be, he had to kill the son that he so urgently desired and whose birth defied the gods—indeed, everyone.

When Oedipus and Laius crossed paths again, it was still Laius who provoked murder. In virtue of his rank and power, and supported by his attendants, he had the solitary youth Oedipus whipped for disrespect in not stepping aside quickly enough to let Laius' carriage pass. If Oedipus had been weaker, it is he who would have died. But it was Oedipus who killed Laius, and we know the price that he had to pay.

However, no one learned from the experience, least of all Oedipus. As a father, he himself cursed his disrespectful sons—with death, even. Since he was fated, how can we reproach him? Unlike Sophocles, Aeschylus was doubtless correct in emphasizing the inevitable train of tragic events that derived from the curse on the House of Labdacus rather than stressing the specific individual drama.

The gods had provided a model: 'Kronos eats his children as the wild boar devours the sows's farrow' (Freud, 1900). Because of a trick, however, one of the sons, Zeus, escaped being swallowed, and was later to defeat Kronos and exile him. Zeus knew what he was doing, for Kronos had, abetted by his mother, similarly dethroned and castrated Uranus, his brutal and implacable paternal tyrant. Although immortals obviously cannot be killed, the desire to kill is still there; this is the source of their life experience. Zeus could therefore be wiser than Laius. Gaea, who was both his grandmother and great-grandmother (in the beginning she alone conceived a son and thereby created a husband), warned him that if Metis had a son after bearing a daughter, he would dethrone Zeus in due time; Zeus therefore swallowed Metis. It was in this way that Athena was born out of the head of Zeus; Athena, armed reason, the virgin who justifies the murder by contraception of a brother always to be born.

A Discussion of the Paper by Helena Besserman Vianna on 'A Peculiar Form of Resistance to Psycho-Analytical Treatment'

Willy Baranger

Willy Baranger

Willy Baranger
Sevilla 2954
Buenos Aires, Argentina

Willy Baranger  

It is well known that Freud's references to the negative therapeutic reaction are not very frequent and that this phenomenon has very seldom been treated in works published after Freud. However, it is worth consideration, for it represents a common cause of failure—or a menace to success—of the analytical process. It is therefore to the credit of Dr Vianna (this issue) to have taken it up, the more so since it gives us an opportunity to relate her observations to our own experience and understanding of the problem.

This confrontation is likely to elicit some surprise among us. The negative therapeutic reaction is a phenomenon we all talk about but which we are in fact far from understanding. In our opinion, the problem should be approached from two different points of view which, while closely related, should be clearly distinguished: first, the delimitation of the phenomenon itself, and second, the processes that underlie or produce the phenomenon. The first process appears to us through our difficulty in clearly determining, at the level of clinical description, those elements that should be ranged under the label of negative therapeutic reaction and those that should be seen as other forms of resistance or other causes for the failure of a psychoanalytical treatment.

We have at our disposal a clear and concrete point of departure: Freud's description of the negative therapeutic reaction as a paradoxical response on the part of the analysand to an interpretation, which constitutes in itself a step forward towards analytic understanding. Instead of improving, as one expects, the state of the patient becomes worse.

However, as far as the concept of negative therapeutic reaction is concerned, there seems to be an evolution parallel to the one we can observe in connexion with other analytical concepts: the phenomenon, clearly defined at the beginning, becomes diluted, gradually losing its specificity and encompassing phenomena that seem to have nothing to do with those that led at the beginning to the formulation of the concept. What it gains in extension, it loses in specificity. At a limit, the concept of negative therapeutic reaction becomes so vague that we can no longer distinguish it from other types of resistance that lead to the failure of analysis.

A Discussion of the Paper by Ralph R. Greenson on 'Transference: Freud or Klein'

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld

Herbert Rosenfeld
9 Meadow Bank
Primrose Hill Road
London NW3 3AY

Herbert Rosenfeld  

I have had the opportunity to study a more detailed version of Dr Greenson's paper (this issue). He claims that he represents the Freudian point of view and he describes what he believes to be the Kleinian approach. He proposes to make a clear distinction between Freudian and Kleinian analysis, concentrating particularly on the understanding and handling of the transference. To understand and present clearly the work of other analysts is a very difficult task, but, in spite of Dr Greenson's knowledge of the literature and his personal contact with Kleinian analysts, he misrepresents what might be considered to be the Kleinian contribution to psychoanalysis. This makes it very difficult to consider his paper as a basis of scientific discussion of differences in analytic theory and practice. Some of Dr Greenson's statements about the Kleinian approach seem to be fragments of case reports or papers and are quite misleading, as they are taken out of their context. In addition, he makes some remarks which are quite meaningless to me and which I cannot relate to my own work or that of my colleagues. He deduces, for example, that Kleinians believe that from the beginning of the analysis the child and adult have a well-developed transference neurosis and a trustworthy working alliance, and therefore that they can intervene at the deepest level from the start. In summing up the differences between the Freudian and Kleinian approach, Greenson emphasizes that in the Freudian technique one works only with the ego and all major therapeutic efforts are aimed directly at the ego. He further states that the Kleinian analyst does not concern himself with the capacity of the patient's ego to cope with interpretations, because the analyst believes that the correct content of the interpretation makes insight instantly therapeutically effective. These and similar distortions I would not consider fruitful to argue about. I would be exceedingly critical of any analyst, Kleinian or not Kleinian, working in this way.

A Discussion of the Paper by Henry Edelheit on 'Crucifixion Fantasies and their Relation to the Primal Scene'

Eugenio Gaddini

Eugenio Gaddini

Eugenio Gaddini
Via Sebastiano Conca 13
00197 Rome
Italy

Eugenio Gaddini  

Dr Edelheit's paper is on a very wide subject, and the way in which this has been treated is very stimulating. The amount of time which I am allowed to discuss it is, on the other hand, relatively limited. I will try to follow up only some of the numerous and very interesting arguments which Dr Edelheit has proposed, but I do not presume to do justice to this work in the way it deserves.

We must in the first place give Dr Edelheit the credit for having called our attention to the fantasies of crucifixion and to their relationship to the primal scene. The evidence of the clinical material presented forces the reader to make an immediate and useful comparison with the clinical material of his own cases. A psychotic woman patient of mine one day got up from the couch and, staring at me in a fright, said, 'But you are Christ, you are really Christ!' Another patient, an obsessional neurotic, dreamt that he had shrunk in size and was crouching down by a shut door, and that there was a crack between the floor and the door which he could see through: first of all he noticed two big bare feet moving about the room, but then he became immediately aware that they emanated light, and he realized with terror that Christ was in the room: there was in the dream the feeling that in a part of the room which could not be seen there must have been the Madonna, and that Christ was moving towards her. Another patient, a character case with serious disturbances of potency and with notable obsessional traits, never parted from a crucifix which his father had held in his hands on his death-bed; at night he kept it in a drawer of his bedside table and during the day in his trouser pocket, so that he could often hold it secretly in his hands. He also used it fairly often in his anal masturbation.

I could add more about these and other cases, but I think that many analysts may have had somewhat similar experiences. What I wish to point out, however, is that the primal scene had greatly influenced the psychopathological structure of each of the patients I mentioned. All this is to say that I cannot but support and confirm Dr Edelheit's findings.

[[RunningHead]]

Panel on 'Hysteria Today'

J. Laplanche

J. Laplanche

J. Laplanche
55 rue de Varenne
Paris 7e

J. Laplanche  

Serge Lebovici introduced the panellists and invited them to give summaries of their papers, which had been precirculated.

The first participant, David Beres, asked several questions arising out of the subject of the panel: If what we call 'hysteria' has changed over the years, what has changed? Is it its clinical aspect, is it our understanding of its psychodynamics, or is it a combination of both?

Regarding the clinical aspect, Beres limited himself to a few remarks. In trying to define the concept of hysteria (an 'ever more amorphous entity', according to Ernst Kris), one meets a further difficulty in that the colloquial use of the term may unwittingly intrude into the writings of even psychiatrists and psychoanalysts.

According to Beres, the patients that we see today are not the same as those described by Charcot, Freud or Breuer. The most obvious clinical difference is that hysteria is today less frequently a symptom condition and more often a character or personality disorder; nor do we see 'pure' hysteria, either as a symptom or as a personality disorder. Hysterical symptoms (or, specifically, conversion symptoms) may be accompanied by phobic, obsessive or compulsive manifestations. Similarly, an apparently hysterical personality may also manifest infantile, narcissistic, paranoid or schizoid traits.

In terms of nosology, however, one must take account of other bodily manifestations, such as anxiety equivalents, organ neuroses, psychosomatic disorders, somatopsychic disorders and hypochondriasis. If we restrict the concept of conversion to changes in body functions that express specific unconscious fantasies and are revealed by psychoanalytic scrutiny, then we must accept that not all functional disturbances or organic changes are hysterical in nature.

Beres devoted the greater part of his paper to the psychodynamic aspects of hysteria. Our advances in hysteria come from the increasing development of psychoanalytic psychology along three lines: (1) the structural theory, (2) the recognition of aggression as an instinctual drive along with libido, and (3) the concept of multiple function.

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by J. Chasseguet-Smirgel on 'Perversion, Idealization and Sublimation'

André Lussier

André Lussier

André Lussier
5786 Avenue Deom
Montreal 251, Canada

André Lussier  

The paper by Mme Chasseguet-Smirgel (this issue) represents a valuable contribution to our psychoanalytic knowledge. It sheds a new light on the role played by anal erotism and idealization in the formation of perversions and of pseudo-creative work, thus extending the line of thought followed by Freud, (1910), (1913), Abraham (1921), Jones (1918), Grunberger (1964) and a few other contributors to this field.

The author brings to the fore, in a most convincing way, the relationship that exists in the fantasy world of the pervert between the ego ideal and anal omnipotence (in my opinion, ego ideal being used here in the sense of Lagache's ideal ego). In a clarity of style worth mentioning as it is in no way irreconcilable with the profundity of her thinking, Mme Chasseguet-Smirgel gives us the main lines of the pervert's dynamic and fantasy history by means of some of its most meaningful manifestations.

Chasseguet-Smirgel's argument is a rigorous one and on the whole carries conviction. It brings up many questions and hypotheses of a major interest. Time permits me to discuss but a few of them.

THE NARCISSISTIC INJURY

If the male child denies the absence of the penis and the presence of the vagina in the mother, can one really say that it is basically as a protection against the narcissistic injury that would result from a confrontation with his own genital inadequacy? This theoretical view, which is shared by Grunberger, does not totally convince me. Of course, I am no judge of my own unconscious prejudices nor of my illusions, but I would rather believe that I am motivated by my clinical experience if I express some doubt and wish to underline another aspect of the unconscious conflict. However, I must admit that I have often had the occasion of observing the pervert's great sensitivity to the thought that the father, whether idealized or not, is the only one genitally equipped to satisfy the mother. But, specifically, the disavowal of the vagina and the repression of the desire to penetrate it have always seemed to me to be linked first and foremost to fantasies having very little to do with the genital capacity proper. The vagina is felt to be an abyss, a terrifying 'chasm' recalling the most archaic fantasies of devouring orality. This fantasy of an annihilating chasm is constantly maintained either by the desire and dread of a regressive return to the maternal womb or by the projection onto the mother of the desire to repossess the penis. To mentally confront the mystery of the maternal body, the child has but his pregenital fantasies at his disposal; he thus finds himself immersed, as in a nightmare, in the darkness of the most primitive fantasies at the time of traumatic confrontations with the mother's genitalia. This is why, in my opinion, he is a prey more to anxiety than to humiliation and more to terror than to impotence.

[[RunningHead]]

The Intuitive Psychoanalytic Perspective of Galdós in Fortunata and Jacinta

George Allison and Joan Ullman

Joan Ullman

George H. Allison
Joan Connelly Ullman
Blakely Psychiatric Group
2271 N.E. 51st Street
Seattle, Washington 98105

Joan Ullman  

SUMMARY

Beginning with the text of this great novel, Fortunata and Jacinta, and singling out for study the developing tragedy of Maximiliano, we began our focus with one aspect of Galdós' creativity: his knowledge of psychosis. In the course of our research, we found that Galdós displays a unique 'Freudian' clinical psychiatric acumen that is woven into the fabric of his novels, which has been noted before by Garma and by non-psychoanalytic authors. Galdós' descriptions of delusions, hallucinations, fantasies and dreams are clinically valid and deeply moving. He demonstrates in his novels an unconscious knowledge of 'the laws, rules and dimensions of the unconscious roots of human behaviour' (Barchilon, 1971). For Galdós, writes Gullón (1960), 'to dream is not to separate oneself from life but to enter it through another door, by means of a shadowy chamber, illuminated for one instant by the ray of light of the dreamer'.

Galdós' portrayal of Maximiliano stands by itself as a major literary creation. Within the structure of the great novel, Maximiliano's gradual retreat from reality adds a major dimension for it both logically develops out of, and contributes to, the plot. It is, in short, a beautifully drawn and at times profoundly poetic characterization of a human being locked in the classic struggle against inner and outer forces he is not able to control. The psychological veracity of this characterization and the clinical validity of the 'case history' are worthy of special note, as are other psychiatric and psychoanalytically astute characterizations in his novels.

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by J. L. Weinberger and J. J. Muller on 'The American Icarus Revisited: Phallic Narcissism and Boredom'

Alan Eisnitz

Alan Eisnitz

Alan J. Eisnitz
125 East 87th Street
New York, N.Y. 10028

Alan Eisnitz  

Boredom arises when the possibility for gratification cannot be experienced. It can occur as a symptom often closely connected with depression. It also occurs as a normal state. It can arise from primarily internal sources, endogenous boredom, or for mainly external reasons, exogenous boredom. Boredom of this second type can be viewed as an affect signal that the external environment offers little promise of gratification and that change should be contemplated although not necessarily affected. Optimum functioning requires of us that we possess the capacity to continue with an activity, when necessary, in spite of boredom. Many tasks, steps towards a desired goal, are in themselves boring.

Libidinal drives appear to possess more plasticity than do the aggressive drives. Variety can be viewed as a characteristic of libidinal drive derivatives, while sameness seems to be more typical of aggressively derived activity. We can assume that the libidinal drives create a pressure for variety particularly in those areas which are more removed from direct instinctual gratification. This is true on the oral level and especially on the genital level. Even in the most constant love relationships variety is sought and even more so in the more sublimated forms of genitality. Only the anal drives, somewhat more limited in terms of anatomical and physiological effector apparatus, seem to show more sameness. This may be one reason why so many anally derived patterns are so useful in dealing with the everyday tasks of the world. Their capacity for sameness helps promote order. However, even on the anal level sublimations show an impressive variety. It seems therefore to be a reasonable assumption that there is drive–ego pressure for variety of discharge. From this point of view boredom can be considered as an affect signal that the potential for varied gratification is not available, whether the reason for this is external or internal.

Pathological boredom is primarily intrapsychic and has an unconscious origin. It may arise from conflict at any libidinal level. While it may be endured, it is difficult to eradicate, even with changes in the external environment.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Provisional Contribution to the Psychoanalytical Study of Time

M. Dupont

M. Dupont

M. A. Dupont
Avenida de las Palmas 755-601
Mexico 10, D.F., Mexico

M. Dupont  

The basic concept of time is related to the occurrence of events, between which, when not simultaneous, there exists a lapse that we call an interval of time. The physical events conventionally chosen to determine the unity of time have been the earth's rotation on its own axis, or its rotation around the sun: solar time. However, other time measures in physics have been developed when they become new verifiable events, as is the case of atomic time, based on the quantum changes of the permanent equilibrium–non-equilibrium of matter (Reichenbach, 1959).

The relativity of time depends both on the occurrence of events that limit their intervals and on the characteristics with which they are registered (Landau & Rumer, 1970). Time sense, i.e. the sense of the passage or happening of time, is a relation between the registrator and his environment.

Within the concept of the passage of time, the present, which separates past from future, is the instant in which what was undetermined becomes determined; therefore the actual happening of an event is equal to becoming determined or fixed in time. If the experience of the human being take place in the present, each thinking act defines a point of reference in time.

In this paper I refer mainly to time as a psychic phenomenon. Freud (1920), (1924), (1925) pointed out that the idea we have of this phenomenon begins in the auto-perception of the alternate psychic energy which, from the unconscious, enables the conscious-perceptual apparatus to register stimuli.

This energy, when placed and withdrawn rhythmically from the unconscious on to the perceptual apparatus, suggests an organically rooted phenomenology that represents the link between biological time rhythms and those of nature in general. The trace of this inherited rhythm is imprinted on each organ of the economy in its physiological work–rest cycles. The inherited phylogenetic rhythm is expressed in the central nervous system in the sleep–wakefulness cycle and also in sleep–dream periodicity.

[[RunningHead]]

A Discussion of the Paper by M. A. Dupont: 'A Provisional Contribution to the Psychoanalytical Study of Time'

Jerome Kavka

Jerome Kavka

Jerome Kavka
180 North Michigan Avenue
Chicago, Ill. 60601

Jerome Kavka  

Even a casual survey of philosophical, scientific and other literature dealing with time concepts reveals what a fascinating, yet mystifying, subject time is (Auger, 1957); (Bridgman, 1961); (Fraser, 1966); (Hofstadter, 1970); (Mach, 1902); (Pearlman, 1969); (Platt, 1968); (Spencer, 1971); (Toulmin & Goodfield, 1965). Our credulity is strained by the 'elastic nature of time as expressed by relativity theory' ('Travellers in Space-time', in The Sciences, New York Academy of Sciences, 1966) and by recent theories of a terminus to space (referred to in the New York Times, 1973), so inextricably linked to time. Closer to our own area of interest, many aspects of human phylogenesis and ontogenesis are as enigmatic to the psychological investigator as are the origins and boundaries of the universe to the physical scientist.

Dr Dupont (this issue) attempts a metapsychology of time based on Melanie Klein's theory of mental development. His conclusions achieve consistency within that frame of reference and, in addition, I believe he forms a bridge with other psychoanalytic theories of mental development. In this sense, his efforts are most welcome. However, since the metapsychology of the psychoanalyst can be distinguished from the philosopher's metaphysics by virtue of being grounded in clinical observation rather than in pure introspection (Brenner, 1970), he needs to enrich his compressed theoretical statement with clinical material from the psychoanalysis of patients. In that case he would have synthesized a metapsychology of time derived from the reciprocal interaction of theory and practice. Until then, we are left with metaphysical propositions whose predictive or therapeutic value is untested.

Strictly speaking, a psychoanalytic study of time would be limited by the confines of its data gathering, i.e. the set of the psychoanalytic treatment situation. Our point of vantage is the clinical experience with our patients and their described subjective variations in time sense. There is already an extensive, if scattered, literature in this area. The use of time references in the patient's associations as a symbolic projection of thought processes also falls into this area. Clinical theories can be derived from these described experiences and, as the literature amply reveals, the therapist's evaluation is made in the context of each therapist's personal theoretical reference frame. What I have discovered is that most time theories derive from work with the usually non-analysable patient in psychotherapy rather than in analysis per se; one reason for this would be the extensive changes reported by narcissistic personalities in their time sense in contrast to the average neurotic patient (Dooley, 1941).
